content:
❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: This problem is unsuited for a Clash of Code, regardless of the mode. The state management and float precision requirements make it too tedious for Fastest, too wordy for Shortest, and nearly impossible to decrypt for a Reverse. However, it would make a great standalone puzzle, where players have the time to properly implement and test the simulation logic.

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: I think this is a good challenge, but I also feel like it would be better suited to Classic Puzzles due to its difficulty and length, as well as traps.

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Great exercise! I really enjoyed the concept of calibrating the nozzle with random movements and the logic of the F, B, H instructions.

Great exercise! I really enjoyed the concept of calibrating the nozzle with random movements and the logic of the F, B, H instructions.
It’s both intuitive to understand and challenging to implement correctly, especially when tracking the total distance and instruction sequence.
The test cases are well thought out, covering realistic situations like overshooting or precise adjustments.
Overall, a very complete and motivating exercise.

This is a great simulation logic, but currently, I am wondering if this is really suitable for CoC ? the story is cute, but pretty long and the strict equality checks on floats is a real trap due to precision errors. It looks more like a classic puzzle to me, as it stands now. For a CoC, maybe you can switch everything to INT (just a suggestion) ?

Some things are less than intuitive to me.
1. The additional CPU-instruction is a nice gotcha but does not add anything.
2. The halt instruction is a bit ambiguous. I would expect it to mean halt - full stop - print the output and exit. Halting 4 times seems redundant. 

Alternatively 'H' could mean 'hold', and continue adjusting if another shift occurs. This would be reactive, and thus swap the timing between instruction and shift. As a bonus, this would solve point 1.

It might be too long reverse mode, but I would like to see what percentage of players can do it in under 15 minutes :)

Other than that; good concept, clear statement, fun puzzle!

The author's solution fails on the following case. In Python, 0.1 + 0.2 evaluates to 0.30000000000000004.

0.0 0.3
2
0.1
0.2

Expected:

FFH
0.300 0.300
Success

Result:

FFB
0.300 0.300
Failure
Good catch @kozuechan. I have edited my solution accordingly and modified Test/Validators 6-7 to catch floating point rounding errors.

Hey @RobbertvZ, thanks for the feedback! I included the additional CPU instruction so that the halt instruction becomes intuitive - once the CPU outputs the halt instruction, all movement should cease. I will consider changing the halt instruction to a hold instruction instead and/or only requiring one 'H' instruction if it makes more sense!

Hey @N3I, thanks for the feedback! I don't think the story is that long but I agree that the equality float check can trip some clashers up (including me, oops). I will consider switching to integers as you mentioned, if there is interest in this clash! Right now, I'm leaning more towards scrapping this idea altogether.
